summary: Yegge analyzes why Borderlands became an enduring addiction despite being a seemingly simple shooter, arguing that its success stems from accidentally creating a perfect 'token economy' through its gun collecting system. He explains that fun alone doesn't create addiction—you need rarity-based collectibles, high score lists, and ways to display your collection. Gearbox stumbled into greatness through two critical bugs (the Armory glitch and ledge glitch) that enabled farming rare 'Pearlescent' weapons, combined with the Playthrough 2.5 endgame mode. The post predicts Borderlands 2 will be great but players will return to the original, and that an MMO version is inevitable.
key_points: Token economies—systems that reward behavior with visible, ranked tokens—are among the most powerful drivers of human behavior, used everywhere from schools to prisons to games
Fun gets people in the door, but addiction requires rarity, stack-ranking, display cases for collections, and personalization
Badge systems are 'what stupid people do when they think they've figured out Token Economies'—tokens need to be scored and ranked to create real addiction
Two accidental bugs (Armory glitch and ledge glitch) transformed Borderlands from a good game into an enduring phenomenon by enabling sustainable farming
The introduction of ultra-rare Pearlescent weapons—born from a color-coding bug players mythologized—gave collectors something worth grinding for years
money_quotes: Token economies are among the most powerful drivers of human behavior. They're used in grade schools, prisons, mental institutions and the military to incent people to act in certain ways. And it works, boy howdy does it ever work. Once they start handing out those gold stars, you'd shoot your own grandmother to get one.
Badge systems are what stupid people do when they think they've figured out Token Economies.

It's a fucking train wreck when the writers are juvenile, because they'll alienate everyone above their own level of sophistication—a demographic that just so happens to have the most disposable income to spend on games.

Fun alone won't keep people playing your game—both offline and online—four years later. Nope. In fact Fun has very little to do with the Magic Recipe for Addiction. All Fun can do for you is get people in the door.
Rarity creates desirability. It's hardwired into the human brain.
Jumping is fun. Period. End of story. If playing your game involves manipulating a humanoid ragdoll in three dimensions, and it doesn't support jumping, then you suck.
What the fuck good is collecting things if you can't show off your collection?
If raiding the Armory is like refined highbrow shopping at Saks Fifth Avenue, then Crawmerax's death is like a bomb going off in a Toys R Us.
key_insight: Addiction in games (and products) comes not from fun but from token economies with rarity, ranking, and display—Borderlands succeeded because bugs accidentally enabled the perfect collecting ecosystem.
